column3: Seeking explanation or confirmation that Mom is the one at fault
column5: えっ、お母さんが悪いの。
column6: Huh, is Mom the one at fault?
column7: Sentence-final の adds an explanatory or confirming tone. Here it sounds like the speaker is reacting with surprise and asking for confirmation.

column3: While I was doing various things, I ended up becoming late
column5: 今日は子供を迎えに行ったり、買い物をしたり、色々やっていたら遅くなりました。
column6: Today I went to pick up the children, did some shopping, and did various other things, so I ended up being late.
column7: Here ていたら gives a when/while I was doing that, it turned out... nuance. It links an ongoing situation or sequence of actions to the result that followed.

column3: If you keep being late, you will not become good at Japanese
column5: いつも遅刻していると、日本語が上手になりませんよ。
column6: If you're always late, you won't get better at Japanese.
column7: Here と expresses a natural or expected result. It presents being late as leading to the consequence that your Japanese will not improve.

column3: Quoting what the teacher said: “You came early this morning.”
column5: 「お母さん、今日ね、『朝早くきたね。』って先生に褒められたよ」
column6: "Mom, today, the teacher praised me, saying, 'You came early this morning.'"
column7: Here って is the casual quotative form of と, used to mark the content of what the teacher said before 褒められた.

column3: "Was praised" in the past passive form
column4: Verb (dictionary form) -> passive 〜(ら)れる -> past 〜(ら)れた
column5: 「お母さん、今日ね、『朝早くきたね。』って先生に褒められたよ」
column6: "Mom, today, the teacher said, 'You came early this morning,' and I was praised."
column7: 褒められた is the past passive form of 褒める. Passive conjugation rule: Group 1 (う-verbs): change the final sound to the あ-row and add れる (書く→書かれる, 読む→読まれる, 話す→話される). Group 2 (る-verbs): remove る and add られる (ほめる→ほめられる, しかる→しかられる, 食べる→食べられる). Group 3 irregular: する→される, くる→こられる. Here 褒める is a Group 2 verb, so 褒める→褒められる, then past tense gives 褒められた. In this sentence, 先生に marks who did the praising.
